When, it falls to the ground the first its gravitational potential energy starts converting into kinetic energy. Due to air friction some kinetic energy losses and converts into heat energy to air. When apple falls on the ground, then its remaining energy converts into heat energy to ground and sound energy. Hence, option (c) is correct.

question: Let us consider a copper wire having radius r and length l. Let its resistance be R. If the radius of another copper wire is 2r and the length is l/2, then the resistance of this wire will be

en.content: Let us consider a copper wire having radius r and length l. Let its resistance be R. If the radius of another copper wire is 2r and the length is l/2, then the resistance of this wire will be
en.explanation:
Given, length of copper wire = l and radius of copper wire = r

$$\therefore$$ Resistance,

$$R = \rho {l \over A} = \rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$ ($$\because$$ A = $$\pi$$r2) ..... (i)

For second wire,

length, $${l_1} = {l \over 2}$$ and radius, r1 = 2r

Resistance of wire,

$${R_1} = \rho .{{{l_1}} \over {\pi {r_1}^2}} = \rho .{{l/2} \over {\pi {{(2r)}^2}}}$$

$$ = \rho .{l \over {2 \times \pi .4{r^2}}} = {1 \over 8}.\rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$

$$ = {1 \over 8}.R$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ = {R \over 8}$$

en.excerpt: Let us consider a copper wire having radius r and length l. Let its resistance be R. If the radius of another copper wire is 2r and the length is l/2, then the resistance of this wire will be

solution:
Given, length of copper wire = l and radius of copper wire = r

$$\therefore$$ Resistance,

$$R = \rho {l \over A} = \rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$ ($$\because$$ A = $$\pi$$r2) ..... (i)

For second wire,

length, $${l_1} = {l \over 2}$$ and radius, r1 = 2r

Resistance of wire,

$${R_1} = \rho .{{{l_1}} \over {\pi {r_1}^2}} = \rho .{{l/2} \over {\pi {{(2r)}^2}}}$$

$$ = \rho .{l \over {2 \times \pi .4{r^2}}} = {1 \over 8}.\rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$

$$ = {1 \over 8}.R$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$ = {R \over 8}$$

question: Two metallic wires A and B are made using copper. The radius of wire A is r while its length is l. A DC voltage V is applied across the wire A, causing power dissipation P. The radius of wire B is 2r and its length is 2l and the same DC voltage V is applied across it causing power dissipation P1. Which one of the following is the correct relationship between P and P1 ?

en.content: Two metallic wires A and B are made using copper. The radius of wire A is r while its length is l. A DC voltage V is applied across the wire A, causing power dissipation P. The radius of wire B is 2r and its length is 2l and the same DC voltage V is applied across it causing power dissipation P<sub>1</sub>. Which one of the following is the correct relationship between P and P<sub>1</sub> ?
en.explanation:
For metallic wire A,

Resistance, RA = $$\rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$

Therefore, power dissipation in wire A, when a DC voltage V is applied,

$$P = {{{V^2}} \over {{R_A}}} = {{{V^2}} \over {\rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}}} = {{{V^2}.\,\pi {r^2}} \over {\rho l}}$$

$$P = {{\pi {r^2}{V^2}} \over {\rho l}}$$ ...... (i)

For metallic wire B,

lB = 2l, rB = 2r, then resistance of wire B,

$${R_B} = \rho .{{{l_B}} \over {\pi {r_B}^2}} = \rho .{{2l} \over {\pi {{(2r)}^2}}} = \rho .{{2l} \over {4\pi {r^2}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Power dissipation in wire B,

$${P_1} = {{{V^2}} \over {{R_B}}} = {{{V^2}} \over {{{\rho \,.\,2l} \over {4\pi {r^2}}}}} = {{2\pi {r^2}{V^2}} \over {\rho l}} = 2P$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$\therefore$$ $$P = {{{P_1}} \over 2}$$

solution:
For metallic wire A,

Resistance, RA = $$\rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$

Therefore, power dissipation in wire A, when a DC voltage V is applied,

$$P = {{{V^2}} \over {{R_A}}} = {{{V^2}} \over {\rho .{l \over {\pi {r^2}}}}} = {{{V^2}.\,\pi {r^2}} \over {\rho l}}$$

$$P = {{\pi {r^2}{V^2}} \over {\rho l}}$$ ...... (i)

For metallic wire B,

lB = 2l, rB = 2r, then resistance of wire B,

$${R_B} = \rho .{{{l_B}} \over {\pi {r_B}^2}} = \rho .{{2l} \over {\pi {{(2r)}^2}}} = \rho .{{2l} \over {4\pi {r^2}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Power dissipation in wire B,

$${P_1} = {{{V^2}} \over {{R_B}}} = {{{V^2}} \over {{{\rho \,.\,2l} \over {4\pi {r^2}}}}} = {{2\pi {r^2}{V^2}} \over {\rho l}} = 2P$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$$\therefore$$ $$P = {{{P_1}} \over 2}$$

question:
Consider the following statements about a solenoid.

1. The magnetic field strength in a solenoid depends upon the number of turns per unit length in the solenoid.

2. The magnetic field strength in a solenoid depends upon the current flowing in the wire of the solenoid.

3. The magnetic field strength in a solenoid depends upon the diameter of the solenoid.

Which of the statements given above are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following statements about a solenoid.

1. The magnetic field strength in a solenoid depends upon the number of turns per unit length in the solenoid.

2. The magnetic field strength in a solenoid depends upon the current flowing in the wire of the solenoid.

3. The magnetic field strength in a solenoid depends upon the diameter of the solenoid.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
en.explanation:
Magnetic field strength in a solenoid is given by

B = $$\mu$$0ni

where, $$\mu$$0 = permeability of free space, n = number of turns per unit length and i = current flowing through solenoid.

So, it is clear that magnetic field strength B in a solenoid depends on n and i only, it does not depends on diameter of solenoid. Therefore, statements 1 and 2 are correct.

So, it is clear that magnetic field strength B in a solenoid depends on n and i only, it does not depends on diameter of solenoid. Therefore, statements 1 and 2 are correct.

question: The focal length of the objective lens of a telescope is 50 cm. If the magnification of the telescope is 25, then the focal length of the eyepiece is

en.content: The focal length of the objective lens of a telescope is 50 cm. If the magnification of the telescope is 25, then the focal length of the eyepiece is
en.explanation:
Given, focal length of objective lens, fo = 50 cm and magnification of telescope m = 25

If fe be the focal length of eyepiece, then

$$m = - {{{f_o}} \over {{f_e}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 25 = - {{50} \over {{f_e}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {f_e} = - {{50} \over {25}} = - 2$$ cm

$$\left| {{f_e}} \right| = 2$$ cm

question: Which one of the following force is non-central and non-conservative?

en.content: Which one of the following force is non-central and non-conservative?
en.explanation:
A force on body which does not always act towards a fixed point, is called non-central force.

The forces that do not store energy are called non-conservative forces.

Work done by non-conservative forces depends on the path of the body moving from one point to another point.

Therefore, frictional force is a non-central and non-conservative force.

en.title: NDA 2019 Paper 1

question:
Consider the following statements relating to Richter scale.

1. It was devised in 1935 by Charles F. Richter.

2. It describes the quantity of energy released by a single earthquake.

3. Richter scale has no upper limit.

Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following statements relating to Richter scale.

1. It was devised in 1935 by Charles F. Richter.

2. It describes the quantity of energy released by a single earthquake.

3. Richter scale has no upper limit.

Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?
en.explanation:
Richter scale is a scale of number used to tell the size of earthquakes.

Charles Francis Richter developed the Richter scale in 1935.

This scale describes the quantity of energy released by a single earthquake.

Numbers for the Richter scale are in range from 0 to 9.

So, here statements (1) and (2) are correct.

question: Suppose there are two planets, 1 and 2, having the same density, but their radii are R1 and R2 respectively, where R1 > R2. The accelerations due to gravity on the surface of these planets are related as

en.content: Suppose there are two planets, 1 and 2, having the same density, but their radii are R<sub>1</sub> and R<sub>2</sub> respectively, where R<sub>1</sub> > R<sub>2</sub>. The accelerations due to gravity on the surface of these planets are related as
en.explanation:
Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of first planet,

$${g_1} = {{G{M_1}} \over {{R_1}^2}}$$ ...... (i)

where, M1 is the mass of first planet.

But, $${M_1} = {4 \over 3}\pi R_1^3.\,{\rho _1}$$ ($$\because$$ mass = volume $$\times$$ density)

where, $$\rho$$1 = density of the first planet.

$${g_1} = {{G\,.\,{4 \over 3}\pi R_1^3.\,{\rho _1}} \over {{R_1}^2}}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$${g_1} = {4 \over 3}G\pi {\rho _1}{R_1}$$ ..... (ii)

Similarly, acceleration due to gravity on the surface of second planet,

$${g_2} = {4 \over 3}G\pi {\rho _2}{R_2}$$ ...... (iii)

$$\therefore$$ From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

$${{{g_1}} \over {{g_2}}} = {{{\rho _1}{R_1}} \over {{\rho _2}{R_2}}} = {{{R_1}} \over {{R_2}}}$$ ($$\because$$ $$\rho$$1 = $$\rho$$2)

Since, R1 > R2, hence g1 > g2.

en.title: NDA 2019 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science
en.excerpt: Suppose there are two planets, 1 and 2, having the same density, but their radii are R1 and R2 respectively, where R1 > R2. The accelerations due to gravity on the surface of these planets are related as

solution:
Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of first planet,

$${g_1} = {{G{M_1}} \over {{R_1}^2}}$$ ...... (i)

where, M1 is the mass of first planet.

But, $${M_1} = {4 \over 3}\pi R_1^3.\,{\rho _1}$$ ($$\because$$ mass = volume $$\times$$ density)

where, $$\rho$$1 = density of the first planet.

$${g_1} = {{G\,.\,{4 \over 3}\pi R_1^3.\,{\rho _1}} \over {{R_1}^2}}$$ [from Eq. (i)]

$${g_1} = {4 \over 3}G\pi {\rho _1}{R_1}$$ ..... (ii)

Similarly, acceleration due to gravity on the surface of second planet,

$${g_2} = {4 \over 3}G\pi {\rho _2}{R_2}$$ ...... (iii)

$$\therefore$$ From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

$${{{g_1}} \over {{g_2}}} = {{{\rho _1}{R_1}} \over {{\rho _2}{R_2}}} = {{{R_1}} \over {{R_2}}}$$ ($$\because$$ $$\rho$$1 = $$\rho$$2)

Since, R1 > R2, hence g1 > g2.

options: apparent shift in the direction of sun
question: The sun is seen little before it rises and for a short while after it sets. This is because of

en.content: The sun is seen little before it rises and for a short while after it sets. This is because of

options.content: apparent shift in the direction of sun

en.explanation:
The sun is seen little before it rises and for short while after it sets due to atmospheric refraction.

When light enters from vacuum to earth's atmosphere, it basically enters from rarer to denser medium and bends towards horizon.

During sunrise, when the sun is just below the horizon, our atmosphere causes the light rays to bend and we see the sun early. Similarly, at sunset, the apparent position of the sun is visible to us and not the actual position due to the same bending of light rays effect.

en.title: NDA 2019 Paper 1

The figure shown below gives the time (t) versus position (x) graph of three objects A, B and C. Which one of the following is the correct relation between their speeds vA, vB and vC, respectively at any instant (t > 0) ?

Image

en.content:
The figure shown below gives the time (t) versus position (x) graph of three objects A, B and C. Which one of the following is the correct relation between their speeds vA, vB and vC, respectively at any instant (t > 0) ?

Image
en.explanation:
From figure,

Image

Velocity of object A,

$${v_A} = {{O{A_1}} \over {A{A_1}}} = \cot {\theta _A}$$

Velocity of object B,

$${v_B} = {{O{B_1}} \over {B{B_1}}} = \cot {\theta _B}$$

Velocity of object C,

$${v_C} = {{O{C_1}} \over {C{C_1}}} = \cot {\theta _C}$$

Since, $${\theta _C} > {\theta _B} > {\theta _A}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cot {\theta _A} > \cot {\theta _B} > \cot {\theta _C}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {v_A} > {v_B} > {v_C}$$

question:
In the given velocity (v) versus time (t) graph, accelerated and decelerated motions are respectively represented by line segments

Image

en.content:
In the given velocity (v) versus time (t) graph, accelerated and decelerated motions are respectively represented by line segments

Image
en.explanation:
The slope of velocity-time graph gives acceleration produced in the moving body.

Image

From graph, slope of line segment AB = tan$$\theta$$2 which is negative because 90$$^\circ$$ < $$\theta$$2 < 180$$^\circ$$

Therefore, line segment AB represents decelerated motion.

Slope of line segment CD = tan$$\theta$$1 which is positive because 0$$^\circ$$ < $$\theta$$1 < 90$$^\circ$$

Therefore, line segment CD represents accelerated motion.

options: The walls of flask are separated by vacuum and made of glass which is a poor conductor of heat.
The glass walls themselves have shiny surfaces.
The surface of inner wall radiates good amount of heat and the surface of outer wall absorbs some of the heat that is radiated from the inner wall.
The cork supports are poor conductors of heat.
question: Which one of the following statement regarding a thermo flask is not correct?

en.content: Which one of the following statement regarding a thermo flask is not correct?

options.content: The walls of flask are separated by vacuum and made of glass which is a poor conductor of heat.
The glass walls themselves have shiny surfaces.
The surface of inner wall radiates good amount of heat and the surface of outer wall absorbs some of the heat that is radiated from the inner wall.
The cork supports are poor conductors of heat.

en.explanation: The thermo flask has the silver coating on the inner surface which prevents heat transfer by radiation. The vacuum between its double wall prevents heat moving by convection. The thinness of the glass walls stops heat entering or leaving the flask by conduction. Hence, option (c) is not a correct statement regarding a thermo flask.

question: The formula for conversion between Fahrenheit and Celsius is $$^\circ$$F = X + (1.8 $$\times$$ $$^\circ$$C).

en.content: The formula for conversion between Fahrenheit and Celsius is $$^\circ$$F = X + (1.8 $$\times$$ $$^\circ$$C).
en.explanation:
The formula for conversion between Fahrenheit and Celsius is

$${{^\circ C} \over 5} = {{^\circ F - 32} \over 9}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {9 \over 5}^\circ C = ^\circ F - 32$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1.8^\circ C = ^\circ F - 32$$

$$ \Rightarrow ^\circ F = 32 + 1.8^\circ C$$

Comparing with given relation of

$$^\circ F = X + (1.8 + ^\circ C)$$

We get, X = 32

options: Any energy transfer that does not involve temperature difference in some way is not heat.
Any energy transfer always requires a temperature difference.
On heating, the length and volume of the object remain exactly the same.
Whenever there is a temperature difference, heat is the only way of energy transfer.
question: Which one of the following statement is correct?

en.content: Which one of the following statement is correct?

options.content: Any energy transfer that does not involve temperature difference in some way is not heat.
Any energy transfer always requires a temperature difference.
On heating, the length and volume of the object remain exactly the same.
Whenever there is a temperature difference, heat is the only way of energy transfer.

en.explanation:
Practically, all the bodies around us either release or absorb energy often in the form of heat. The form of energy which is exchanged among various bodies or system on account of temperature difference is defined as heat.

On heating the matter, the energy of atoms increases and hence the average distance between them increases. This results in thermal expansion.

Hence, option (a) is the correct statement.

On heating the matter, the energy of atoms increases and hence the average distance between them increases. This results in thermal expansion.

Hence, option (a) is the correct statement.

question: If T is the time period of an oscillating pendulum, then which one of the following statement is not correct?

en.content: If T is the time period of an oscillating pendulum, then which one of the following statement is not correct?

options.content: The motion repeats after time T only once.
T is the least time after which motion repeats itself.
The motion repeats itself after nT, where n is a positive integer.
T remains the same only for small angular displacements.

en.explanation:
Time period (T) is the time taken by the particle to complete one oscillation. Time period of the simple pendulum is given by

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {{l \over g}} $$

It is clear that the time period is independent of the mass of the pendulum. This formula is valid only when radius of point mass of pendulum is negligible compared to length of pendulum.

Here, motion of pendulum repeats itself after a fixed interval, i.e. it remains same only for small angular displacements (Q). Hence, it is incorrect to say that this motion repeats after time T only once.

options: Its motion is along a straight line.

question: If an object moves with constant velocity, then which one of the following statement is not correct?

en.content: If an object moves with constant velocity, then which one of the following statement is not correct?

options.content: Its motion is along a straight line.
Its displacement increases linearly with time.

en.explanation:
Whenever an object moves with constant velocity, the motion is always in form of straight line, which is parallel to the X-axis. As velocity remains same, hence acceleration of the object is zero. Also, the speed of the object changes due to change in displacement, so than in distance.

Hence, option (d) is incorrect because it is not necessary displacement increases linearly with time. It decreases linearly for constant velocity.

question: An object is moving with uniform acceleration a. Its initial velocity is u and after time t, its velocity is v. The equation of its motion is v = u + at. The velocity (along Y - axis) - time (along X-axis) graph will be a straight line

en.content: An object is moving with uniform acceleration a. Its initial velocity is u and after time t, its velocity is v. The equation of its motion is v = u + at. The velocity (along Y - axis) - time (along X-axis) graph will be a straight line
en.explanation:
From the graph for the equation of its motion,

v = (u + at)

Image

The object has an initial velocity u at point A and then its velocity changes at a uniform rate from A to B in time t. In other words, there is a uniform acceleration a from A to B and after time t its final velocity becomes v at B in the graph. Hence, the slope of a velocity-time graph is equal to acceleration.

Thus, we can say that the velocity-time graph for the equation, v = u + at will be straight line having slope equal to acceleration a with y intercept u.

Hence, option (c) is correct.

question: What is the net force experienced by a bar magnet placed in a uniform magnetic field?

en.content: What is the net force experienced by a bar magnet placed in a uniform magnetic field?

options.content: Depends upon length of the magnet

en.explanation:
The correct answer should be zero. When a bar magnet or magnetic dipole is placed in a uniform magnetic field, it will not experience only force, but it is bound to experience a torque.

This torque will align the dipole moment of the bar magnet to align along the direction of magnetic field. However, if a bar magnet is placed in a non-uniform magnetic field, then it will be subjected to a force and torque.

question: Which one of the following has maximum inertia?

en.content: Which one of the following has maximum inertia?
en.explanation:
Inertia means resistance or opposition offered by the body to change its state. Mass of a body is the measurement of its inertia. A body with greater mass shows greater inertia, i.e. it is more difficult to change its state of rest or uniform motion as compared to that of a body having small mass.

Hence, in the given option a cricket ball has maximum mass, so it has maximum inertia.

options: It is experienced by all bodies in the universe.

question: Which one of the following statement about gravitational force is not correct?

en.content: Which one of the following statement about gravitational force is not correct?

options.content: It is experienced by all bodies in the universe.
It is a dominant force between celestial bodies.
It is a negligible force for atoms.
It is same for all pairs of bodies in our universe.

en.explanation:
Gravitational force is defined as a non-contact force of attraction between any two bodies in universe (no matter how far the bodies are).

It is experienced by all bodies in the universe. The celestial bodies experienced these forces, but it is not same for all pairs of bodies in our universe.

So, option (d) is not a correct statement.

question: Whether an object will float or sink in a liquid, depends on

en.content: Whether an object will float or sink in a liquid, depends on

options.content: mass of the object and density of liquid only
difference in the densities of the object and liquid
mass and shape of the object only

en.explanation:
Whether an object sinks or floats depends on the density of the object and the fluid in which it is immersed.

An object that is denser than a fluid will sink in the fluid while an object that is less dense will float. A floating object is said to be buoyant.

The classical Greek inventor Archimedes' was first to understand that buoyancy is a force.

Archimedes' principle states that any object immersed in or floating in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of displaced fluid.

question: Which one of the following device is non-ohmic?

en.content: Which one of the following device is non-ohmic?
en.explanation:
According to Ohm's law, the electric current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference applied across its ends, provided the physical condition (such as temperature) remains unchanged.

Hence, the device which follows Ohm's law for all voltage across it is called as an ohmic device. For example, a wire rheostat conducting copper coil, electric heating coil, etc.

The device that does not follow Ohm's law is known as non-ohmic device. For example, semi-conductor diode.

question:
Which of the following statement(s) about a fluid at rest in a cup is/are correct ?

1. Pressure is same at all the points in the fluid.

2. Pressure is exerted on the walls.

3. Pressure exists everywhere in the fluid.

en.content:
Which of the following statement(s) about a fluid at rest in a cup is/are correct ?

1. Pressure is same at all the points in the fluid.

2. Pressure is exerted on the walls.

3. Pressure exists everywhere in the fluid.
en.explanation:
When a fluid is at rest in a cup then pressure is same at all the points in the fluid, pressure is exerted on the walls and pressure exists everywhere in the fluid.

The pressure at a point within a volume of fluid means that the point to be surrounded by a small container by the area of the container.

Hence, all the statements are correct.

question:
Which of the following statement(s) about latent heat for a given substance is/are correct?

1. It is fixed at a given temperature.

2. It depends upon the temperature and volume.

3. It is independent of temperature and volume.

4. It depends on the temperature but independent of volume.

en.content:
Which of the following statement(s) about latent heat for a given substance is/are correct?

1. It is fixed at a given temperature.

2. It depends upon the temperature and volume.

3. It is independent of temperature and volume.

4. It depends on the temperature but independent of volume.
en.explanation: The amount of heat per unit mass transferred during change of state of the substance is called latent heat of the substance. It is always fixed at a given temperature and does not change with temperature and volume of the substance, i.e. it is independent of temperature and volume. e.g. Latent heat of ice is 3.34 $$\times$$ 10<sup>5</sup> J/kg. So, option (b) is the correct answer.

question: A ball balanced on a vertical rod is an example of

en.content: A ball balanced on a vertical rod is an example of
en.explanation:
When the centre of gravity of a body lies below point of suspension or support, the body is said to be in stable equilibrium. When the centre of gravity of a body lies above the point of suspension or support, the body is said to be in unstable equilibrium.

A balanced ball on a vertical rod is an example of unstable equilibrium because when the rod is slightly disturbed its centre of gravity is lowered.

The line of action of its weight lies outside the base of the rod. Then, the torque due to weight, toppled it down.

When the centre of gravity of a body lies at the point of suspension, the body is said to be inventral equilibrium.

options.content: It can be either positive or negative.
It is unity for all materials.
It is always greater than one.

en.explanation: Refractive index of a material is given by the ratio of speed of light in vacuum to the speed of light in any medium. Refractive index is always greater than one because by the definition of refractive index, (n) of a medium (e.g. glass) is the ratio of the speed of light (c), in a vacuum to the speed in the medium v and speed of light is maximum in vacuum, which gives the value of n greater than one.

They do not cross each other.
Field lines between two poles cannot be precisely straight lines at the ends.

question: The magnetic field strength of a current carrying wire at a particular distance from the axis of the wire

en.content: The magnetic field strength of a current carrying wire at a particular distance from the axis of the wire

options.content: depends upon the current in the wire
depends upon the radius of the wire
depends upon the temperature of the surroundings

en.explanation:
The magnetic field strength of a current carrying wire at a particular distance from the axis of the wire is given by the Biot-Savart's law.

Magnetic field, $$dB \propto {{idl\sin \theta } \over {{r^2}}}$$

where, i = current in wire

and r = distance of the point from the wire.

dB $$\propto$$ i

Hence, option (a) is correct.

question: A stainless steel chamber contains Ar gas at a temperature T and pressure p. The total number of Ar atoms in the chamber is n. Now, Ar gas in the chamber is replaced by CO2 gas and the total number of CO2 molecules in the chamber is n/2 at the same temperature T. The pressure in the chamber now is p'. Which one of the following relation holds true? (Both the gases behave as an ideal gas)

en.content: A stainless steel chamber contains Ar gas at a temperature T and pressure p. The total number of Ar atoms in the chamber is n. Now, Ar gas in the chamber is replaced by CO<sub>2</sub> gas and the total number of CO<sub>2</sub> molecules in the chamber is n/2 at the same temperature T. The pressure in the chamber now is p'. Which one of the following relation holds true? (Both the gases behave as an ideal gas)
en.explanation:
For Ar gas $$pV = nRT$$

$$p = {{nRT} \over V}$$ .... (i)

For CO2 gas, $$n' = {n \over 2},p = p'$$

$$p'V = n'RT \Rightarrow p' = {n \over 2}{{RT} \over V}$$

From Eq. (i), we get

$$p' = {p \over 2}$$

For CO2 gas, $$n' = {n \over 2},p = p'$$

$$p'V = n'RT \Rightarrow p' = {n \over 2}{{RT} \over V}$$

From Eq. (i), we get

$$p' = {p \over 2}$$

question:
Consider the following circuit.

Image

Which one of the following is the value of the resistance between points A and B in the circuit given above ?

en.content:
Consider the following circuit.

Image

Which one of the following is the value of the resistance between points A and B in the circuit given above ?
en.explanation:
According to question,

Image

In the above circuit, R1 and R2 are connected in the parallel combination.

Hence, equivalent resistance,

$${1 \over {R'}} = {1 \over R} + {1 \over R} \Rightarrow R' = {R \over 2}$$

Now, circuit becomes

Image

In the circuit, R' and R are connected in series combination. Hence,

$$R'' = R/2 + R = {{R + 2R} \over 2} \Rightarrow R'' = {{3R} \over 2}$$

Now, R'' and R4 are connected in parallel combination. Hence, equivalent resistance between A and B is

$${1 \over {{R_{AB}}}} = {1 \over {{{3R} \over 2}}} + {1 \over R}$$

$${1 \over {{R_{AB}}}} = {2 \over {3R}} + {1 \over R} \Rightarrow {1 \over {{R_{AB}}}} = {{2 + 3} \over {3R}}$$

$${1 \over {{R_{AB}}}} = {5 \over {3R}} \Rightarrow {R_{AB}} = {3 \over 5}R$$

question:
Consider the following statements about visible light, UV light and X-ray.

1. The wavelength of visible light is more than that of X-ray.

2. The energy of X-ray photons is higher than that of UV light photons.

3. The energy of UV light photons is less than that of visible light photons.

Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following statements about visible light, UV light and X-ray.

1. The wavelength of visible light is more than that of X-ray.

2. The energy of X-ray photons is higher than that of UV light photons.

3. The energy of UV light photons is less than that of visible light photons.

Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?
en.explanation:
(1) The frequency of X-ray is greater than visible light. So, the wavelength of visible light is more than that of X-rays.

(2) We know that, E = h$$\nu $$

Energy (E) $$\propto$$ Frequency ($$\nu $$), X-rays have higher frequency, so it has higher energy than that of UV light photons.

(3) UV light have higher frequency, so it has higher energy than that of visible light.

Hence, statements 1 and 2 both are correct but statement 3 is incorrect.

en.title: NDA 2018 Paper 2
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

solution:
(1) The frequency of X-ray is greater than visible light. So, the wavelength of visible light is more than that of X-rays.

(2) We know that, E = h$$\nu $$

Energy (E) $$\propto$$ Frequency ($$\nu $$), X-rays have higher frequency, so it has higher energy than that of UV light photons.

(3) UV light have higher frequency, so it has higher energy than that of visible light.

Hence, statements 1 and 2 both are correct but statement 3 is incorrect.

question: The time period of oscillation of a simple pendulum having length L and mass of the bob m is given as T. If the length of the pendulum is increased to 4L and the mass of the bob is increased to 2 m, then which one of the following is the new time period of oscillation?

en.content: The time period of oscillation of a simple pendulum having length L and mass of the bob m is given as T. If the length of the pendulum is increased to 4L and the mass of the bob is increased to 2 m, then which one of the following is the new time period of oscillation?
en.explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum,

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {{L \over g}} $$ .... (i)

According to the question, the length of the pendulum increased to 4L, i.e.

L' = 4L

New, time period of simple pendulum,

$$T' = 2\pi \sqrt {{{4L} \over g}} $$

$$ = 2 \times \left( {2\pi \sqrt {{L \over g}} } \right)$$

From Eq. (i), we get

T' = 2T

question:
Consider the following statements about a microscope and a telescope.

1. Both the eyepiece and the objective of a microscope are convex lenses.

2. The focal length of the objective of a telescope is larger than the focal length of its eyepiece.

3. The magnification of a telescope increases with the increase in focal length of its objective.

4. The magnification of a microscope increases with the increase in focal length of its objective.

Which of the statement(s) given above are correct?

en.content:
Consider the following statements about a microscope and a telescope.

1. Both the eyepiece and the objective of a microscope are convex lenses.

2. The focal length of the objective of a telescope is larger than the focal length of its eyepiece.

3. The magnification of a telescope increases with the increase in focal length of its objective.

4. The magnification of a microscope increases with the increase in focal length of its objective.

Which of the statement(s) given above are correct?
en.explanation:
(1) Microscope consists of two convex lenses co-axially separated by some distance. The lens nearer to the object is called the objective. The lens through which the final image is viewed is called the eyepiece.

(2) The focal length of the objective of a telescope of eyepiece because objective has much larger aperture than the eyepiece.

(3) Magnification of telescope is directly proportional to the focal length of objective (fo). Hence, the focal length of telescope increases with the increase in focal length of its objective.

Hence, statements (1), (2) and (3) all are correct.

(2) The focal length of the objective of a telescope of eyepiece because objective has much larger aperture than the eyepiece.

(3) Magnification of telescope is directly proportional to the focal length of objective (fo). Hence, the focal length of telescope increases with the increase in focal length of its objective.

Hence, statements (1), (2) and (3) all are correct.

question:
A planet has a mass M1 and radius R1. The value of acceleration due to gravity on its surface is g1.

There is another planet 2, whose mass and radius both are two times that of the first planet. Which one of the following is acceleration due to gravity on the surface of planet 2 ?

en.content:
A planet has a mass M1 and radius R1. The value of acceleration due to gravity on its surface is g1.

There is another planet 2, whose mass and radius both are two times that of the first planet. Which one of the following is acceleration due to gravity on the surface of planet 2 ?
en.explanation:
Acceleration due to gravity at planet is given by

$${g_1} = {{G{M_1}} \over {{R_1}^2}}$$ .... (i)

For the second planet,

M2 = 2M1, R2 = 2R1

Now, acceleration due to gravity,

$${g_2} = {{G(2{M_1})} \over {{{(2{R_1})}^2}}} \Rightarrow {g_2} = {1 \over 2} \times {{G{M_1}} \over {{R_1}^2}}$$

From Eq. (i), we get

$${g_2} = {{{g_1}} \over 2}$$

question:
A ball is released from rest and rolls down an inclined plane as shown in the following figure, requiring 4 s to cover a distance of 100 cm along the plane

Image

Which one of the following is the correct value of angle $$\theta$$ that the plane makes with the horizontal ? (g = 1000 cm/s2)

en.content:
A ball is released from rest and rolls down an inclined plane as shown in the following figure, requiring 4 s to cover a distance of 100 cm along the plane

Image

Which one of the following is the correct value of angle $$\theta$$ that the plane makes with the horizontal ? (g = 1000 cm/s2)
en.explanation:
Given, initial velocity of ball, u = 0

distance, s = 100 cm and time, t = 4 s

We know that,

$$s = ut + {1 \over 2}a{t^2}$$

$$100 = 0 + {1 \over 2} \times a \times {(4)^2}$$

$$a = {{100 \times 2} \over {4 \times 4}} = {{25} \over 2}$$ cm/s2

Now, a = g sin$$\theta$$

$$\sin \theta = {a \over g} = {{25/2} \over {1000}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {{1 \over {80}}} \right)$$

distance, s = 100 cm and time, t = 4 s

question: The coefficient of areal expansion of a material is 1.6 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$5 K$$-$$1. Which one of the following gives the value of coefficient of volume expansion of this material?

en.content: The coefficient of areal expansion of a material is 1.6 $$\times$$ 10<sup>$$-$$5</sup> K<sup>$$-$$1</sup>. Which one of the following gives the value of coefficient of volume expansion of this material?
en.explanation:
We know that,

$${{area\,{\mathop{\rm expansion}\nolimits} \,coefficient\,(\beta )} \over {volumetric\,{\mathop{\rm expansion}\nolimits} \,coefficient\,(\gamma )}} = {2 \over 3}$$

$$\gamma = {3 \over 2} \times \beta $$, where $$\beta$$ = 1.6 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$5 K$$-$$1

$$\gamma = {3 \over 2} \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 5}}$$ K$$-$$1

$$\gamma$$ = 2.4 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$5 K$$-$$1

en.excerpt: The coefficient of areal expansion of a material is 1.6 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$5 K$$-$$1. Which one of the following gives the value of coefficient of volume expansion of this material?

solution:
We know that,

$${{area\,{\mathop{\rm expansion}\nolimits} \,coefficient\,(\beta )} \over {volumetric\,{\mathop{\rm expansion}\nolimits} \,coefficient\,(\gamma )}} = {2 \over 3}$$

$$\gamma = {3 \over 2} \times \beta $$, where $$\beta$$ = 1.6 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$5 K$$-$$1

$$\gamma = {3 \over 2} \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 5}}$$ K$$-$$1

$$\gamma$$ = 2.4 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$5 K$$-$$1

question: The refractive indices of two media and denoted by n1 and n2 and the velocities of light in these two media are respectively, v1 and v2. If n2/n1 is 1.5, then which one of the following statement is correct?

en.content: The refractive indices of two media and denoted by n<sub>1</sub> and n<sub>2</sub> and the velocities of light in these two media are respectively, v<sub>1</sub> and v<sub>2</sub>. If n<sub>2</sub>/n<sub>1</sub> is 1.5, then which one of the following statement is correct?
en.explanation:
We know that,

Refractive index

= $${{Velocity\,in\,vacuum\,(c)} \over {Velocity\,in\,medium\,(v)}}$$

In first medium, $${n_1} = {c \over {{v_1}}}$$ .... (i)

Similarly, $${n_2} = {c \over {{v_2}}}$$ ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${{{n_2}} \over {{n_1}}} = {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}}$$

$$1.5 = {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}} \Rightarrow {v_1} = 1.5{v_2}$$

Hence, option (a) is correct.

en.title: NDA 2018 Paper 2
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science
en.excerpt: The refractive indices of two media and denoted by n1 and n2 and the velocities of light in these two media are respectively, v1 and v2. If n2/n1 is 1.5, then which one of the following statement is correct?

solution:
We know that,

Refractive index

= $${{Velocity\,in\,vacuum\,(c)} \over {Velocity\,in\,medium\,(v)}}$$

In first medium, $${n_1} = {c \over {{v_1}}}$$ .... (i)

Similarly, $${n_2} = {c \over {{v_2}}}$$ ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${{{n_2}} \over {{n_1}}} = {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}}$$

$$1.5 = {{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}} \Rightarrow {v_1} = 1.5{v_2}$$

Hence, option (a) is correct.

question: A circular coil of radius R having N number of turns carries a steady current I. The magnetic induction at the centre of the coil is 0.1 T. If the number of turns is doubled and the radius is halved, then which one of the following will be the correct value for the magnetic induction at the centre of the coil?

en.content: A circular coil of radius R having N number of turns carries a steady current I. The magnetic induction at the centre of the coil is 0.1 T. If the number of turns is doubled and the radius is halved, then which one of the following will be the correct value for the magnetic induction at the centre of the coil?
en.explanation:
Magnetic field at the centre of coil,

$$B = {{{\mu _0}NI} \over {2r}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 0.1 = {{{\mu _0}NI} \over {2r}}$$ .... (i)

According to the question,

$$N' = 2N,r' = {r \over 2}$$

Magnetic inductance at the centre of the coil,

$$B' = {{{\mu _0}N'I} \over {2r'}}$$

$$ = {{{\mu _0}(2N)(I)} \over {2 \times {r \over 2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow B' = 4\left( {{{{\mu _0}NI} \over {2r}}} \right)$$

From Eq. (i), we get

B' = 4 $$\times$$ 0.1

$$\Rightarrow$$ B' = 0.4 T

Magnetic field at the centre of coil,

$$B = {{{\mu _0}NI} \over {2r}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 0.1 = {{{\mu _0}NI} \over {2r}}$$ .... (i)

From Eq. (i), we get

B' = 4 $$\times$$ 0.1

$$\Rightarrow$$ B' = 0.4 T

en.content: Water boils at a lower temperature at high altitudes, because
en.explanation: Water boils at a lower temperature at high altitudes, because the air pressure is less. When atmospheric pressure is lower, it takes less energy/heat to bring water to the boiling point, which means water will boil at a lower temperature at high altitudes.

en.content: Concave mirror is used in headlights of vehicles, because it

options.content: focuses light from the bulb onto nearly vehicles
fits well into the shape of the headlight

en.explanation:
When an object is kept at the focus of a concave mirror, the light rays after reflection from it travels parallel to each other.

Therefore, the bulb of the headlight is kept at the focus of the concave mirror, so it allows the light to spread out (sends parallel) to infinity (longer distance).

question: If some object is weighed when submerged in water, then what will happen to its weight compared to its weight in air?

en.content: If some object is weighed when submerged in water, then what will happen to its weight compared to its weight in air?

options.content: Increases or decreases cannot be predicted

en.explanation: If some object is weighed when submerged in water, then weight of object will decrease as compared to its weight in air. This is explained by Archimedes' principle. According to this, if an object is immersed in a fluid, then it experiences an apparent loss of weight which is equal to the weight of fluid displaced.

en.content: An object moves in a circular path with a constant speed. Which one of the following statements is correct?

The centripetal acceleration is the same for both the gentle and sharp curves.
The centripetal acceleration causes the object to slow down.

question: The force acting on a particle of mass m moving along the X-axis is given by F(x) = Ax2 $$-$$ Bx. Which one of the following is the potential energy of the particle?

en.content: The force acting on a particle of mass m moving along the X-axis is given by F(x) = Ax<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ Bx. Which one of the following is the potential energy of the particle?
en.explanation:
Potential energy,

$$U(x) = - \int_{{x_0}}^x {F(x)dx} $$ (For one-dimension motion)

Given, $$F(x) = A{x^2} - Bx$$

$$ = - \int_0^x {(A{x^2} - Bx)dx} $$

$$ = - \left[ {{{A{x^3}} \over 3} - {{B{x^2}} \over 2}} \right] = - \left[ {{{2A{x^3} - 3B{x^2}} \over 6}} \right]$$

$$ = - {{{x^2}} \over 6}[2Ax - 3B]$$

Potential energy,

Given, $$F(x) = A{x^2} - Bx$$

$$ = - \int_0^x {(A{x^2} - Bx)dx} $$

$$ = - {{{x^2}} \over 6}[2Ax - 3B]$$

question: How long does light take to reach the earth from the sun?

en.content: How long does light take to reach the earth from the sun?
en.explanation:
Distance of earth from sun

= 150 million kilometre

or 149600000 km

Speed of light

= 3.0 $$\times$$ 108 m/s = 3 $$\times$$ 105 km/s

Thus, the time that light takes to reach the earth from the sun = $${{149600000} \over {3 \times {{10}^5}}}$$ = 498.66 s or 8 min 20 sec (approx.)

question: The majority charge carriers in a p-type semiconductors are

en.content: The majority charge carriers in a p-type semiconductors are
en.explanation: Silicon and germanium doped with electron deficit impurities are called p-type semiconductors. When Si or Ge is doped with group 13 elements like B or Al which contains only 3 valence electrons in the place of fourth electron, a hole is created. This hole in turn is filled up by an electron from some other site and so on. The motion of charges in this case is more conveniently thought of as the motion of the holes. Therefore, the majority charge carriers are holes.

question: The ionisation energy of hydrogen atom in the ground state is

en.content: The ionisation energy of hydrogen atom in the ground state is
en.explanation:
The ionisation energy, $$E = {{13.6\,{Z^2}} \over {{n^2}}}eV$$

For hydrogen atom, Z = 1 and for ground state, n = 1

$$\therefore$$ Energy, $$E = {{13.6} \over 1}eV = 13.6\,eV$$

Hence, the ionisation energy of hydrogen atom in the ground state is 13.6 eV.

question: Which one of the following devices changes low voltage alternating current to high voltage alternating current and vice-versa?

en.content: Which one of the following devices changes low voltage alternating current to high voltage alternating current and vice-versa?
en.explanation:
Transformer is an electronic device which changes low voltage to high voltage alternative current and vice-versa. This change of voltage is obtained by two types of transformers.

(i) Step-up transformer

(ii) Step-down transformer

In step-up transformer, the number of turns in secondary coil NS > NP (Number of turns in primary coil), which increases the voltage.

Similarly in step-down transformer NS < NP, which decreases the voltage.

question: An optical illusion which occurs mainly in deserts during hot summer is based on the principle of

en.content: An optical illusion which occurs mainly in deserts during hot summer is based on the principle of
en.explanation:
An optical illusion in deserts is based on the phenomenon of Total Internal Reflection (TIR) of light.

It is the phenomenon in which when light rays incident on a refracting medium at an angle greater than the critical angle, whole of the light is reflected back to the same medium. Therefore, when the layers of air close to the ground have varying temperature with hottest layers near the ground, light from a distant tree may undergo total internal reflection and the apparent image of the tree may create an illusion to the observer.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists.

Image

en.content:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists.

Image
en.explanation:
Hypermetropia In this defect, the person cannot seer near object clearly and image is formed behind the retina. This defect can be corrected by using convex lens.

Presbyopia In old age, the power of accommodation of the eye lens decrease, therefore neither near nor distant objects are clearly seen. It is corrected by using bifocal lens.

Myopia In this defect, the person cannot see the distant object clearly and image is formed before the retina. This defect can be corrected by using concave lens.

Cataract In this defect, an opaque white membrane is developed on cornea due to which a person losses power of vision partially or completely. This defect can be removed by removing this membrane through surgery.

So, option (a) is the correct nswer.

question: A circular coil of single turn has a resistance of 20$$\Omega$$. Which one of the following is the correct value for the resistance between the ends of any diameter of the coil?

en.content: A circular coil of single turn has a resistance of 20$$\Omega$$. Which one of the following is the correct value for the resistance between the ends of any diameter of the coil?
en.explanation:
The resistance of single turn of coil is 20 $$\Omega$$.

Image

The resistance of half semicircle ACB and ADB will be 10 $$\Omega$$ each.

Diagonally, they are in parallel, so the equivalent resistance,

$${1 \over {{R_{AB}}}} = {1 \over {{R_{ACB}}}} + {1 \over {{R_{ADB}}}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {10}} + {1 \over {10}} = {2 \over {10}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ RAB = 5 $$\Omega$$

question: In a solenoid, the current flowing through the wire is I and number of turns per unit length is n. This gives a magnetic field B inside the solenoid. If number of turns per unit length is increased to 2n, what will be the value of magnetic field in the solenoid?

en.content: In a solenoid, the current flowing through the wire is I and number of turns per unit length is n. This gives a magnetic field B inside the solenoid. If number of turns per unit length is increased to 2n, what will be the value of magnetic field in the solenoid?
en.explanation:
The magnetic field inside the solenoid,

B = $$\mu$$0nI,

so, B $$\propto$$ n

When the number of turns per unit length is increased to 2n, then the field B' $$\propto$$ 2n

$$\therefore$$ B' = 2B

options: Magnification increases with the increase in focal length of eyepiece.
Magnification increases with the increase in focal length of objective.

question: Which one of the following statements is correct about the magnification of an optical microscope?

en.content: Which one of the following statements is correct about the magnification of an optical microscope?

options.content: Magnification increases with the increase in focal length of eyepiece.
Magnification increases with the increase in focal length of objective.
Magnification does not depend upon the focal length of eyepiece.
Magnification decreases with the increase in focal length of eyepiece.

en.explanation:
The magnification of compound microscope is given by the following formula, $$m = \left( {1 - {{{v_o}} \over {{f_o}}}} \right)\left( {1 + {D \over {{f_e}}}} \right)$$

where,

vo = object distance,

fo = focal length of objective,

fe = focal length of eyepiece

and D = least distance of distant vision.

$$m \propto {1 \over {{f_e}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Magnification will decrease with the increase in the focal length of the eyepiece.

en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

question: Colour vision in human eyes is the function of photoreceptor cells named

en.content: Colour vision in human eyes is the function of photoreceptor cells named
en.explanation:
Day light vision (photopic) or colour vision in human eyes is the function of photoreceptor cells named cones. Three types of cone cells present in human eye that contain photoreceptive pigments for red, green and blue lights.

Rods are the other category of photoreceptor cells for twilight vision (scotopic vision).

Blind spot is the point at which the optic nerve (transmits visual information from retina to the brain) leaves the eye. It contains no rods and cones, so an image formed at this point is not sent to the brain.

Fovea is a tiny pit located in the macula of the retina that provides the clearest vision of all.

question: The radii of curvature of the faces of a double convex lens are 10 cm and 20 cm. The refractive index of the glass is 1.5. What is the power of this lens?

en.content: The radii of curvature of the faces of a double convex lens are 10 cm and 20 cm. The refractive index of the glass is 1.5. What is the power of this lens?
en.explanation:
From lens maker's formula,

$${1 \over f} = (\mu - 1)\left( {{1 \over {{R_1}}} - {1 \over {{R_2}}}} \right)$$

Here, $$\mu$$ = 1.5, R1 = 10 cm, R2 = $$-$$20 cm

$${1 \over f} = (1.5 - 1)\left( {{1 \over {10}} + {1 \over {( - 20)}}} \right)$$

$$ = 0.5\left( {{1 \over {10}} + {1 \over {20}}} \right) = 0.5\left( {{3 \over {20}}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow f = {{200} \over {15}}$$ cm

$$\therefore$$ Power of lens,


$$ = {{15 \times 100} \over {200}} = $$ + 7.5 D

question: The time period of a simple pendulum made by using a thin copper wire of length L is T. Suppose the temperature of the room in which this simple pendulum is placed increases by 30$$^\circ$$C. What will be the effect on the time period of the pendulum?

en.content: The time period of a simple pendulum made by using a thin copper wire of length L is T. Suppose the temperature of the room in which this simple pendulum is placed increases by 30$$^\circ$$C. What will be the effect on the time period of the pendulum?

options.content: T will become more than 2 times

en.explanation:
The time period of simple pendulum is given by expression

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt {{L \over g}} $$, where L = length of wire.

Also, when the temperature is changed, the length is given as

Lt = L0 (1 + $$\alpha$$ $$\Delta$$t)

So, Lt $$\propto$$ $$\Delta$$t

where, $$\Delta$$t is increase in temperature.

$$\therefore$$ As the temperature increases, the length also increases.

As, $$T' = 2\pi \sqrt {{{{L_t}} \over g}} \Rightarrow T' \propto L_t^{{1 \over 2}}$$

$$\therefore$$ T will increase slightly.

question: Which one of the following physical quantities does not affect the resistance of a cylindrical resistor?

en.content: Which one of the following physical quantities does not affect the resistance of a cylindrical resistor?

options.content: The resistivity of the material used in the resistor.
The area of cross-section of the cylinder.

en.explanation:
The resistance of a resistor is given by $$R = \rho {l \over A}$$, i.e. R depends on length (l), area of cross-section (A) and nature of material ($$\rho$$).

Hence, current does not affect the resistance of a cylindrical resistor.

question: A Kelvin thermometer and a Fahrenheit thermometer both give the same regarding for a certain sample. What would be the corresponding reading in a Celsius thermometer?

en.content: A Kelvin thermometer and a Fahrenheit thermometer both give the same regarding for a certain sample. What would be the corresponding reading in a Celsius thermometer?
en.explanation:
Let the common temperature in Kelvin and Fahrenheit is X, then

$${{F - 32} \over {180}} = {{K - 273} \over {100}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{X - 32} \over {180}} = {{X - 273} \over {100}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{X - 32} \over 9} = {{X - 273} \over 5}$$

On solving, X = 574

In Celsius thermometer,

K = C + 273

$$\Rightarrow$$ C = K $$-$$ 273

= 574 $$-$$ 273 = 301

question: If the potential difference applied to an X-ray tube is doubled while keeping the separation between the filament and the target as same, what will happen to the cut-off wavelength?

en.content: If the potential difference applied to an X-ray tube is doubled while keeping the separation between the filament and the target as same, what will happen to the cut-off wavelength?

options.content: Will be four times of the original wavelength

en.explanation:
The cut-off wavelength of X-ray,

$${\lambda _{\min }} = {{12375} \over V}\mathop A\limits^o $$ [$$\because$$ $${\lambda _{\min }} \propto {1 \over V}$$]

According to question, if the potential difference is doubled $$\lambda$$min will be halved.

$$\lambda {'_{\min }} \propto {1 \over {2V}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\lambda {'_{\min }} = {1 \over 2}{\lambda _{\min }}$$

en.title: NDA 2017 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

question: If the absolute refractive indices of glass and water are $${3 \over 2}$$ and $${4 \over 3}$$ respectively, what will be the ratio of velocity of light in glass and water?

en.content: If the absolute refractive indices of glass and water are $${3 \over 2}$$ and $${4 \over 3}$$ respectively, what will be the ratio of velocity of light in glass and water?
en.explanation:
Given, $$\mu$$g = 3/2 and $$\mu$$w = 4/3

$$\because$$ $${\mu _g} = {c \over {{v_g}}}$$, $${\mu _w} = {c \over {{v_w}}}$$

where, vg = velocity of light in glass and vw = velocity of light in water.

$$ \Rightarrow {{{\mu _g}} \over {{\mu _w}}} = {{{v_w}} \over {{v_g}}} \Rightarrow {{3/2} \over {4/3}} = {{{v_w}} \over {{v_g}}}$$

$${9 \over 8} = {{{v_w}} \over {{v_g}}}$$ or $${{{v_g}} \over {{v_w}}} = {8 \over 9}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ vg : vw = 8 : 9

en.title: NDA 2017 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science
en.excerpt: If the absolute refractive indices of glass and water are $${3 \over 2}$$ and $${4 \over 3}$$ respectively, what will be the ratio of velocity of light in glass and water?

solution:
Given, $$\mu$$g = 3/2 and $$\mu$$w = 4/3

$$\because$$ $${\mu _g} = {c \over {{v_g}}}$$, $${\mu _w} = {c \over {{v_w}}}$$

where, vg = velocity of light in glass and vw = velocity of light in water.

$$ \Rightarrow {{{\mu _g}} \over {{\mu _w}}} = {{{v_w}} \over {{v_g}}} \Rightarrow {{3/2} \over {4/3}} = {{{v_w}} \over {{v_g}}}$$

$${9 \over 8} = {{{v_w}} \over {{v_g}}}$$ or $${{{v_g}} \over {{v_w}}} = {8 \over 9}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ vg : vw = 8 : 9

question: A positive charge +q is placed at the centre of a hollow metallic sphere of inner radius a and outer radius b. The electric field at a distance r from the centre is denoted by E. In this regard, which one of the following statements is correct?

en.content: A positive charge +q is placed at the centre of a hollow metallic sphere of inner radius a and outer radius b. The electric field at a distance r from the centre is denoted by E. In this regard, which one of the following statements is correct?
en.explanation: The electric field inside a hollow metallic sphere is zero at every point because from Gauss' law, all the charges must reside on the outer surface of the conductor (sphere), hence no charge is present inside the sphere, i.e. E = 0, for r < a.

en.title: NDA 2017 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

solution: The electric field inside a hollow metallic sphere is zero at every point because from Gauss' law, all the charges must reside on the outer surface of the conductor (sphere), hence no charge is present inside the sphere, i.e. E = 0, for r < a.

This circuit consists of two sections, I and II. In first section, three resistors are in parallel connection, therefore equivalent resistance is

$$\therefore$$ Equivalent resistance of the circuit,

Req = R1 + 8

$$ = {{12} \over {11}} + 8 = {{100} \over {11}} = 9{1 \over {11}}\Omega $$

question: A racing car accelerates on a straight line from rest to a speed of 50 m/s in 25 s. Assuming uniform acceleration of the throughout, the distance covered in this will be

en.content: A racing car accelerates on a straight line from rest to a speed of 50 m/s in 25 s. Assuming uniform acceleration of the throughout, the distance covered in this will be
en.explanation:
Acceleration of the car is uniform. Therefore, according to first equation of motion

v = u + at

where, v = final velocity of the car = 50 m/s,

a = acceleration of the car

t = time interval = 25 s

and u = initial velocity = 0.

$$\therefore$$ 50 = 0 + a $$\times$$ 25

$$\Rightarrow$$ a = 2 m/s2

Now, applying second equation of motion, we get

$$s = ut + {1 \over 2}a{t^2}$$

$$ = 0 + {1 \over 2}a{t^2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times 2 \times 25 \times 25$$

$$ = 625$$ m

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

$$\Rightarrow$$ a = 2 m/s2

Now, applying second equation of motion, we get

$$ = 0 + {1 \over 2}a{t^2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times 2 \times 25 \times 25$$

$$ = 625$$ m

question: A given conductor carrying a current of 1 ampere. It produces an amount of heat equal to 2000 J. If the current through the conductor is doubled, then the amount of heat produced will be

en.content: A given conductor carrying a current of 1 ampere. It produces an amount of heat equal to 2000 J. If the current through the conductor is doubled, then the amount of heat produced will be
en.explanation:
Let resistance of the coil is R and measuring heat produced for time t seconds.

$$\therefore$$ H = I2RT = (1)2 (R)(t) $$\Rightarrow$$ 2000 J = Rt

Similarly, when current is doubled, the amount of heat produced is

H' = (I')2Rt = (22)(Rt) = 4Rt

= 4 $$\times$$ 2000 J = 8000 J ($$\because$$ Rt = 2000 J)

question: A lady is standing in front of a plane mirror at a distance of 1 m from it. She walks 60 cm towards the mirror. The distance of her image now from herself (ignoring the thickness of the mirror) is

en.content: A lady is standing in front of a plane mirror at a distance of 1 m from it. She walks 60 cm towards the mirror. The distance of her image now from herself (ignoring the thickness of the mirror) is
en.explanation:
Initial distance of the lady from the mirror is

x1 = 1 m = 100 cm

After moving 60 cm towards the mirror, new distance, x2 = 100 $$-$$ 60 = 40 cm

$$\therefore$$ Distance of her image from herself,

d = x2 + x2 = 2x2 = 80 cm

question: A glass vessel is filled with water up to the brim and a lid is fixed to it tightly. Then it is kept inside a freezer for hours. What is expected to happen?

en.content: A glass vessel is filled with water up to the brim and a lid is fixed to it tightly. Then it is kept inside a freezer for hours. What is expected to happen?

options.content: The water freezes to ice and the level of ice comes down.
The water in the glass vessel summer freezes to ice.
The glass vessel breaks due to expansion as water freezes to ice.
The water does not freeze at all.

en.explanation: Water shows anomalous behaviour in expansion during temperature range of 4$$^\circ$$C to 0$$^\circ$$C. When temperature falls below 4$$^\circ$$C, water expands. As lid is fixed tightly, stress will develop in the bottle due to this anomalous expansion which breaks the glass when water is converted into ice that is volume is maximum.

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

solution: Water shows anomalous behaviour in expansion during temperature range of 4$$^\circ$$C to 0$$^\circ$$C. When temperature falls below 4$$^\circ$$C, water expands. As lid is fixed tightly, stress will develop in the bottle due to this anomalous expansion which breaks the glass when water is converted into ice that is volume is maximum.

question: A simple circuit contains a 12 V battery and bulb having 24 $$\Omega$$ resistance. When turn on the switch, the ammeter connected to the circuit would read

en.content: A simple circuit contains a 12 V battery and bulb having 24 $$\Omega$$ resistance. When turn on the switch, the ammeter connected to the circuit would read
en.explanation:
Given, emf of the battery, E = 12 V and resistance of the bulb, R = 24 $$\Omega$$ where, switch is turned on, current through the bulb is

$$I = {V \over R} = {{12} \over {24}} = 0.5A$$

Hence, reading of ammeter is 0.5 A

question: Three resistors with magnitudes 2, 4 and 8$$\Omega$$ are connected in parallel, then equivalent resistance of the system would be

en.content: Three resistors with magnitudes 2, 4 and 8$$\Omega$$ are connected in parallel, then equivalent resistance of the system would be
en.explanation:
Equivalent resistance of three resistors of resistances 2, 4 and 8 $$\Omega$$,

$${1 \over {{R_{eq}}}} = {1 \over 2} + {1 \over 4} + {1 \over 8} = {{4 + 2 + 1} \over 8} = {7 \over 8}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${R_{eq}} = {8 \over 7}\Omega = 1.142\Omega $$

Therefore, equivalent resistance is less than 2$$\Omega$$.

question: Two bodies A and B are moving with equal velocities. The mass of B is doubled that of A. In this context, which one of the following statement is correct?

en.content: Two bodies A and B are moving with equal velocities. The mass of B is doubled that of A. In this context, which one of the following statement is correct?

options.content: Momentum of B will be double that of A.
Momentum of A will be double that of B.
Momentum of B will be four times that of A.
Momentum of both A and B will be equal.

en.explanation:
Let magnitude of the velocity of each body is v.

$$\therefore$$ Momentum of A, pA = mAv

Similarly, momentum of B, pB = mBv

$$\therefore$$ $${{{p_A}} \over {{p_B}}} = {{{m_A}} \over {{m_B}}} = {{{m_A}} \over {2{m_A}}}$$ ($$\because$$ $${m_B} = 2{m_A}$$)

$$ \Rightarrow {p_B} = 2{p_A}$$

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science
en.excerpt: Two bodies A and B are moving with equal velocities. The mass of B is doubled that of A. In this context, which one of the following statement is correct?

Similarly, momentum of B, pB = mBv

question: An object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave mirror of focal length 16 cm. If the object is shifted by 8 cm towards the focus, then the nature of the image would be

en.content: An object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave mirror of focal length 16 cm. If the object is shifted by 8 cm towards the focus, then the nature of the image would be
en.explanation:
Focal length of the concave mirror, f = $$-$$16 cm

$$\therefore$$ Magnitude of radius of curvature of the mirror, | R | = | 2f | = 32 cm

When object is shifted towards the focus, new distance of the object

u = $$-$$ (32 $$-$$ 8) cm = $$-$$ 24 cm

Applying mirror formula, we get

$${1 \over v} + {1 \over u} = {1 \over f} \Rightarrow {1 \over v} - {1 \over {24}} = {1 \over { - 16}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over v} = {1 \over {24}} - {1 \over {16}}$$

$$ = {{16 - 24} \over {24 \times 16}} = {{ - 8} \over {24 \times 16}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ v = $$-$$48 cm

$$\therefore$$ Magnification, $$m = - {v \over u} = {{ - ( - 48)} \over { - 24}} = - 2$$

As magnification is negative and having magnitude more than one, image will be real and magnified.

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science

When object is shifted towards the focus, new distance of the object

u = $$-$$ (32 $$-$$ 8) cm = $$-$$ 24 cm

Applying mirror formula, we get

$${1 \over v} + {1 \over u} = {1 \over f} \Rightarrow {1 \over v} - {1 \over {24}} = {1 \over { - 16}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over v} = {1 \over {24}} - {1 \over {16}}$$

$$ = {{16 - 24} \over {24 \times 16}} = {{ - 8} \over {24 \times 16}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ v = $$-$$48 cm

$$\therefore$$ Magnification, $$m = - {v \over u} = {{ - ( - 48)} \over { - 24}} = - 2$$

As magnification is negative and having magnitude more than one, image will be real and magnified.

question: A pencil is placed upright at a distance 10 cm from a convex lens of focal length 15 cm. The nature of the image of the pencil will be

en.content: A pencil is placed upright at a distance 10 cm from a convex lens of focal length 15 cm. The nature of the image of the pencil will be
en.explanation:
It is given that,

Focal length of the convex lens, f = 15 cm

Distance of pencil from the pole, u = $$-$$10 cm

Applying mirror formula, we get

$${1 \over v} + {1 \over u} = {1 \over f}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over v} - {1 \over {10}} = {1 \over {15}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over v} = {1 \over {10}} + {1 \over {15}} = {{25} \over {150}}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ v = 6 cm

$$\therefore$$ Magnification, $$m = - {v \over u} = {{ - 6} \over { - 10}} = 0.6$$

As magnification is positive and less than one, so image will be virtual, erect and diminished (reduced).

question: Which one of the following is a conventional energy source?

en.content: Which one of the following is a conventional energy source?
en.explanation:
Bio-mass energy covers entire life of our planet. This type of energy has been used, since the cave-men discovered fire. Bio-mass fuel comes from things that were once living. e.g. Wood product, dried vegetation, crop residue, aquatic plants, etc.

Tidal energy This is the form of hydropower that converts the energy obtained from tides into useful forms of power. It is non-conventional energy.

Geothermal energy This is heat that comes from the sub-surface of the earth. It is contained in the rocks and fluid beneath the earth's crust.

Solar energy It is a non-conventional source of energy. It refers to capturing the energy from the sun and subsequently converting it into electricity. It is simply the light and heat that come from the sun.

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science
en.excerpt: Which one of the following is a conventional energy source?

solution:
Bio-mass energy covers entire life of our planet. This type of energy has been used, since the cave-men discovered fire. Bio-mass fuel comes from things that were once living. e.g. Wood product, dried vegetation, crop residue, aquatic plants, etc.

Tidal energy This is the form of hydropower that converts the energy obtained from tides into useful forms of power. It is non-conventional energy.

Geothermal energy This is heat that comes from the sub-surface of the earth. It is contained in the rocks and fluid beneath the earth's crust.

Solar energy It is a non-conventional source of energy. It refers to capturing the energy from the sun and subsequently converting it into electricity. It is simply the light and heat that come from the sun.

question: A container is first filled with water and then the entire water is replaced by mercury. Mercury has a density of 13.6 $$\times$$ 103 kg/m3. If X is the weight of the water and Y is the weight of the mercury, then

en.content: A container is first filled with water and then the entire water is replaced by mercury. Mercury has a density of 13.6 $$\times$$ 10<sup>3</sup> kg/m<sup>3</sup>. If X is the weight of the water and Y is the weight of the mercury, then
en.explanation:
Let volume of the container is V.

Density of mercury,

$$\rho$$m = 13.6 $$\times$$ 103 kg/m3

We know that, density of water

$$\rho$$w = 103 kg/m3

Volume of container remains same in both the cases.

$$\therefore$$ $${X \over {{\rho _w}}} = {Y \over {{\rho _m}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow Y = \left( {{{{\rho _m}} \over {{\rho _w}}}} \right)X$$

$$ \Rightarrow Y = \left( {{{13.6 \times {{10}^3}} \over {{{10}^3}}}} \right)X = 13.6X$$

Let volume of the container is V.

Density of mercury,

$$\rho$$m = 13.6 $$\times$$ 103 kg/m3

We know that, density of water

$$\rho$$w = 103 kg/m3

Volume of container remains same in both the cases.

$$\therefore$$ $${X \over {{\rho _w}}} = {Y \over {{\rho _m}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow Y = \left( {{{{\rho _m}} \over {{\rho _w}}}} \right)X$$

$$ \Rightarrow Y = \left( {{{13.6 \times {{10}^3}} \over {{{10}^3}}}} \right)X = 13.6X$$

en.content: Density of water is
en.explanation:
Water shows anomalous behaviour between temperatures 0$$^\circ$$C and 4$$^\circ$$C. It expands when temperature goes below 4$$^\circ$$C.

Therefore, density decreases below 4$$^\circ$$C. Also, it expands when temperature goes above 4$$^\circ$$C. Therefore, density of water is maximum (1000 kg m$$-$$3) at 4$$^\circ$$C because volume of water is minimum at 4$$^\circ$$C.

question: Suppose the force of gravitation between two bodies of equal masses is F. If each mass is doubled keeping the distance of separation between them unchanged, then the force would become

en.content: Suppose the force of gravitation between two bodies of equal masses is F. If each mass is doubled keeping the distance of separation between them unchanged, then the force would become
en.explanation:
Let mass of each body is m and separation between the bodies is r.

Gravitational force between the bodies,

$$F = {{G{m^2}} \over {{r^2}}}$$

After doubling the mass, gravitational force becomes,

$$F' = {{G{{(2m)}^2}} \over {{r^2}}} = {{4G{m^2}} \over {{r^2}}} = 4F$$

options: lose one-fourth of its total energy
lose one-fourth of its potential energy
gain one-fourth of its potential energy
gain three-fourth of its total energy
question: A body has a free fall from a height of 20 m. After falling through a distance of 5 m, then body would

en.content: A body has a free fall from a height of 20 m. After falling through a distance of 5 m, then body would

options.content: lose one-fourth of its total energy
lose one-fourth of its potential energy
gain one-fourth of its potential energy
gain three-fourth of its total energy

en.explanation:
Initial gravitational potential energy of the body,

U1 = mgh = 20 mg ($$\because$$ h = 20 m)

$$\therefore$$ Initial total energy of the body,

E = U1 + K1 = U1 + 0 = U1 = 20 mg

After falling through 5 m, then the new potential energy is

U2 = mgh' = mg(20 $$-$$ 5) = 15 mg

$$\therefore$$ Loss in potential energy,

$$\Delta$$U = U1 $$-$$ U2 = 20 mg $$-$$ 15 mg = 5 mg

$$\therefore$$ Ratio of potential energy

$$ = {U \over {\Delta U}} = {{20} \over 5} = {4 \over 1}$$

Hence, body would lose one-fourth of its potential energy.

en.title: NDA 2016 Paper 1
en.subTitle: Physics • General Science
en.excerpt: A body has a free fall from a height of 20 m. After falling through a distance of 5 m, then body would

solution:
Initial gravitational potential energy of the body,

U1 = mgh = 20 mg ($$\because$$ h = 20 m)

$$\therefore$$ Initial total energy of the body,

E = U1 + K1 = U1 + 0 = U1 = 20 mg

After falling through 5 m, then the new potential energy is

U2 = mgh' = mg(20 $$-$$ 5) = 15 mg

$$\therefore$$ Loss in potential energy,

$$\Delta$$U = U1 $$-$$ U2 = 20 mg $$-$$ 15 mg = 5 mg

$$\therefore$$ Ratio of potential energy

$$ = {U \over {\Delta U}} = {{20} \over 5} = {4 \over 1}$$

Hence, body would lose one-fourth of its potential energy.

question:

1. is the amount of matter present in it

2. does not vary from place to place

en.content:
Mass of a particular amount of substance

1. is the amount of matter present in it

2. does not vary from place to place

3. changes with change in gravitational force

Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
en.explanation:
Mass of a particular amount of substance is the amount of matter present in it. It is the inherent property of the substance and does not depend on location of the substance.

Therefore, mass of the substance is independent of gravitational force between them.

question: Which one of the following statement with regarding to expansion of materials due to heating is not correct?

en.content: Which one of the following statement with regarding to expansion of materials due to heating is not correct?

options.content: As ice melts, it expands uniformly upto 4$$^\circ$$C.
Mercury thermometer works using the principle of expansion due to heating.
Small gap is kept between two rails to allow for expansion due to heating.
The length of the metallic wire increases when its temperature is increased.

en.explanation:
Generally, substances expand on heating and contract on cooling. Now, if temperature of certain amount of water is increased from 0$$^\circ$$C to 100$$^\circ$$C, its volume decreases between 0$$^\circ$$C (ice) to 4$$^\circ$$C and increases from 4$$^\circ$$C to 100$$^\circ$$C.

Therefore, during 0$$^\circ$$C to 4$$^\circ$$C, anomalous behaviour of water is observed, i.e. as ice melts, it expands uniformly upto 4$$^\circ$$C.

question: Which one of the following is not a form of stored energy?

en.content: Which one of the following is not a form of stored energy?
en.explanation:
Potential energy of a system is the stored energy associate with the configuration of the system. Electrical energy of a system is also stored energy in the form of electrostatic potential energy. Similarly, chemical energy is stored in the form of chemical potential. Potential energy associated with the chemical bonds, but nuclear energy is obtained by breaking a heavy nucleus into light nucleus of comparable masses. It can also be obtained by the formation of a heavy nucleus due to fusion of two light nuclei.

Therefore, it is not a form of stored energy.

question: When three resistors, each having resistance r, are connected in parallel, their resultant resistance is x. If these three resistances are connected in series, then total resistance will be

en.content: When three resistors, each having resistance r, are connected in parallel, their resultant resistance is x. If these three resistances are connected in series, then total resistance will be
en.explanation:
According to question,

In parallel combination,

$${1 \over r} + {1 \over r} + {1 \over r} = {1 \over x}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3 \over r} = {1 \over x}$$

$$ \Rightarrow r = 3x$$

If the same resistances are connected in series, then R = r1 + r2 + r3

R = 3x + 3x + 3x

R = 9x

question: How is the kinetic energy of a moving object affected if the net work done on it is positive?

en.content: How is the kinetic energy of a moving object affected if the net work done on it is positive?
en.explanation:
When force F acts on an object in such a direction that it makes an angle with the direction of displacement, then

W = Fs cos$$\theta$$

Image

When force and displacement are in the same direction, then work done will be positive.

From work-energy theorem,

Work done = Change in kinetic energy

Wnet = $$\Delta$$ KE = KEf $$-$$ KEi

Net positive work done : Increase in KE.

Net negative work done : Decrease in KE.

question: Which one of the following four particles, whose displacement x and acceleration ax are related as follows, is executing simple harmonic motion?

en.content: Which one of the following four particles, whose displacement x and acceleration a<sub>x</sub> are related as follows, is executing simple harmonic motion?
en.explanation:
Displacement of the particles in SHM,

x = Ax sin($$\omega$$t + $$\phi$$)

Then, for SHM, acceleration of the particles,

$${a_x} = {{dv} \over {dt}} = {d \over {dt}}\left( {{{dx} \over {dt}}} \right)$$ ($$\because$$ $${{dv} \over {dt}} = {{dx} \over {dt}}$$)

$${a_x} = - {A_x}{\omega ^2}\sin (\omega t + \phi )$$ [$$\because$$ $$x = {A_x}\sin (\omega t + \phi )$$]

$${a_x} = - {\omega ^2}x$$

$$\therefore$$ $${a_x} = - 3x$$

question: If we plot a graph between volume V and inverse of pressure p (i.e. $${1 \over p}$$) for an ideal gas at constant temperature T, the curve so obtained is

en.content: If we plot a graph between volume V and inverse of pressure p (i.e. $${1 \over p}$$) for an ideal gas at constant temperature T, the curve so obtained is
en.explanation:
At a given temperature, the volume of a given mass of a gas in inversely proportional to its pressure. This is the statement of Boyle's law.

i.e. $$V \propto {1 \over p}$$

pV = constant

If temperature T remains constant, the process is isothermal. Graph between p and V at constant temperature is isothermal and is an equilateral hyperbola. By plotting p versus 1/V, this hyperbola is converted into a straight line.

question: A particle executes linear simple harmonic motion with amplitude of 2 cm. When the particle is at 1 cm from the mean position, the magnitudes of the velocity and the acceleration are equal. Then, its time period is

en.content: A particle executes linear simple harmonic motion with amplitude of 2 cm. When the particle is at 1 cm from the mean position, the magnitudes of the velocity and the acceleration are equal. Then, its time period is
en.explanation:
The magnitude of velocity and acceleration of particle in SHM when displaced by y, is $$\omega \sqrt {({A^2} - {y^2})} $$ and $${\omega ^2}y$$, respectively.

Equating it, $$\omega \sqrt {({A^2} - {y^2})} = {\omega ^2}y$$

$$\omega = {{\sqrt {{A^2} - {y^2}} } \over y} = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} - {{(1)}^2}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {4 - 1} = \sqrt 3 $$

Time period, $$T = {{2\pi } \over \omega } = {{2\pi } \over {\sqrt 3 }}s$$

$$ = \sqrt {4 - 1} = \sqrt 3 $$

question: When a ray of light enters a glass slab, then

en.content: When a ray of light enters a glass slab, then
en.explanation:
As the light enters from rarer medium to the denser medium, the speed of light decreases but the frequency remains the same. The relation between speed of light, wavelength and the frequency is given as

v = n$$\lambda$$

where, v is the speed of light, n is the frequency of light and $$\lambda$$ is the wavelength of light.

$$\therefore$$ $$n = {v \over \lambda }$$

In denser medium, n remains constant and v decreases. Therefore, $$\lambda$$ also decreases to keep the frequency constant.

where, v is the speed of light, n is the frequency of light and $$\lambda$$ is the wavelength of light.

$$\therefore$$ $$n = {v \over \lambda }$$

In denser medium, n remains constant and v decreases. Therefore, $$\lambda$$ also decreases to keep the frequency constant.

question: Pressure is a scalar quantity because

en.content: Pressure is a scalar quantity because

options.content: it is the ratio of force to area and both force and area are vectors
it is the ratio of magnitude of force to area
it is the ratio of component of force (normal to area) to area

en.explanation:
Pressure (p) = $${{Force(F)} \over {Area(A)}}$$

It is the ratio of magnitude of force to area. Force and area, both are vector quantities and ratio of two vector quantities will always be a scalar quantity.

The SI unit of pressure is N/m2 or pascal.

question: A person is unable to read a newspaper without his glasses. He is most probably suffering from

en.content: A person is unable to read a newspaper without his glasses. He is most probably suffering from
en.explanation: A person is unable to read a newspaper without his glasses because he is probably suffering from presbyopia. It is found in old age people. In this defect, one cannot read comfortably and clearly. For most old age people, the near point gradually recedes away. This defect can be corrected by using bifocal lenses.
